name: Shinto Cosmology
shortDescription: Shinto cosmology describes the creation and structure of the universe, encompassing a multi-layered world with divine and earthly realms. It emphasizes the interconnectedness of all things and the presence of kami (spirits) throughout creation.
description: Shinto cosmology outlines the origin and structure of the universe according to Shinto beliefs. Initially, there was a state of formless chaos. From this chaos emerged Takamagahara, the High Heavenly Plain, home to the first kami. Below Takamagahara existed a murky, chaotic realm. From this chaos, the world gradually formed, separating into distinct realms. The creation myth, as detailed in the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki, describes the divine couple Izanagi and Izanami descending from Takamagahara to create the islands of Japan. Using the Amenonuhoko (heavenly jeweled spear), they stirred the primordial ocean, and when they lifted the spear, the brine dripping from it formed the first island, Onogoro-shima. They then proceeded to create the other islands of Japan and many of the kami who inhabit the world. The earthly realm, Ashihara no Nakatsukuni (the Central Land of Reed Plains), is the world inhabited by humans. Beneath this lies Yomi, the underworld, a dark and impure realm. Shinto cosmology emphasizes the cyclical nature of life and death and the importance of purity and harmony. Kami are believed to inhabit various aspects of nature and human life, and rituals are performed to appease and communicate with them. The concept of Musubi, the generative and harmonizing power, is central to Shinto cosmology, representing the interconnectedness of all things and the continuous cycle of creation and renewal.
